MILESTONE 2 REPORT
 Predicting Student Performance Using OULAD
 Hierarchical Classification: Fail | Good | Very Good | Excellent
 
 Team ID: 60

 Machine Learning — Classification Project

Table of Contents
 1. Introduction & Project Evolution
 2. Preprocessing Differences: Milestone 1 vs Milestone 2
 3. Missing Value Handling Differences
 4. Feature Engineering
 5. Hierarchical Classification Pipeline
 6. Model Results & Comparison
 7. Full Model Report
 8. Discussion & Conclusion

1. Introduction & Project Evolution
Milestone 1 approached this dataset as a regression problem, predicting the numeric assessment score
(0–100) using 30 engineered features, prior performance metrics, VLE click behaviour, and ensemble
regressors (Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM, Stacking). The best result was R²=0.451, ACC@10=0.631.
Milestone 2 reformulates the task as a 4-class classification problem. Since the Milestone 2 dataset replaces
the numeric score with a categorical assessmentClass column (Fail / Pass / Distinction / No Submission),
regression is no longer appropriate. The target is engineered into four performance bands:
Class
Source
Meaning
Fail
final_result = Fail or Withdrawn
Did not successfully complete the module
Good
final_result = Pass (inconsistent)
Passed but with higher fail_rate or late_rate
Very Good
final_result = Pass (consistent)
Passed with fail_rate < 0.3 AND late_rate < 0.4
Excellent
final_result = Distinction
Highest performance band
A hierarchical two-stage pipeline was designed: Stage 1 predicts binary Pass/Fail, and its output is injected
as an extra feature into Stage 2, which predicts the full 4-class label. This decomposition achieves 80.13%
4-class accuracy with the champion HistGBM model.

2. Preprocessing Differences: Milestone 1 vs Milestone 2
The two milestones differ substantially in preprocessing strategy due to the fundamental change from a
regression target (numeric score) to a classification target (assessmentClass).
2.1 Target Variable Construction
Aspect
Milestone 1 (Regression)
Milestone 2 (Classification)
Target variable
Numeric score (0–100)
4-class label: Fail / Good / Very Good / Excellent
Target source
studentAssessments.score (dropped rows with '?')
assessmentClass column; Withdrawn→Fail, Pass→Good/VeryGood, Distinction→Excellent
Missing target
Rows dropped (cannot impute regression target)
No Submission filled as 'No Submission' class; no rows dropped
Target engineering
None — raw score used directly
add_four_class_target() splits Pass into Good/VeryGood using fail_rate and late_rate thresholds
Stage 1 target
N/A
Binary: Fail vs Pass (all passing classes collapsed to Pass)
2.2 Encoding Strategy
Feature group
Milestone 1
Milestone 2
Categorical (OHE)
code_module (removed), assessment_type, code_presentation
code_module, code_presentation, gender, region, disability — handle_unknown='ignore'
Categorical (Ord)
highest_education, imd_band, age_band (integer-coded 0–N)
Same columns but OrdinalEncoder with unknown_value=-1 for unseen categories
Numeric
MinMaxScaler on all 30 features after engineering
RobustScaler only for distance-based models (SVC, LR, SGD); trees use raw numerics
Binary
disability and gender as 0/1
disability and gender kept as string categories, encoded by OHE pipeline
Rare category merge
No formal quals and Post-grad merged manually
Same merging inside reusable preprocess_df() function applied consistently to train/test
Age band merge
Manual: 35-55 and 55+ → 35+
Same, inside preprocess_df()
2.3 Feature Selection & Noise Removal
Milestone 1 applied a structured 5-step feature selection pipeline ending with 30 features after correlation
pruning and 5-fold stability selection. Milestone 2 takes a different approach: rather than formal selection, the
Stage 2 ultimate model explicitly identifies and drops noisy features based on domain knowledge:
Dropped from Stage 2: studied_credits, module_presentation_length, num_of_prev_attempts, disability,
age_band, gender, total_click — replaced by richer engineered signals (avg_hw_score, clicks_early,
clicks_late, avg_days_late_TMA, avg_days_late_CMA).

3. Missing Value Handling Differences
Column / Issue
Milestone 1 Treatment
Milestone 2 Treatment
Score / assessmentClass
Rows with '?' dropped (target must be known)
assessmentClass NaN → 'No Submission' string class; no row loss
imd_band
Filled with column median (ordinal integer)
Filled per-region using the most frequent band from the training set. imd_mode_map learned on train only to prevent leakage
date_registration
Filled with 0 for missing
Filled with date_unregistration minus training-set median offset. reg_date_median learned on train only
total_click
Filled with 0
Filled with 0 (students with no VLE records had 0 interactions)
late_rate
Filled with 0
Filled with 1.0 (students with no submissions have 100% late rate — worst case)
fail_rate
Filled with 0
Filled with 1.0 (students with no submissions have 100% fail rate — worst case)
performance_level
N/A (not used in M1)
Filled with 'No Submission'; then dropped after 4-class target is built to prevent leakage
Missing input columns
Columns pre-built before modelling; no runtime guard
predict_from_raw_csvs() auto-joins missing columns from info/reg/courses tables; remaining unknowns filled with NaN and imputed by preprocess_df()
Train/test consistency
Scaler fit on train only (MinMaxScaler)
All imputation statistics (reg_date_median, imd_mode_map) fit on train only and saved in preprocessing_bundle.pkl for reuse at inference time
Key leakage prevention in Milestone 2: The add_four_class_target() function uses fail_rate and late_rate to
assign the Very Good label, then both columns are dropped before any model sees them. Stage 2 training uses
cross_val_predict on Stage 1 (not predict) so Stage 2 never sees inflated in-sample Stage 1 predictions.

4.1 Base Features (both milestones)

4.2 Advanced Features Engineered in Stage 2 (Milestone 2 only)
Feature
Engineering Logic
Why it helps

Captures different behaviour for automated vs tutor-marked work

Gives Stage 2 a strong prior about the student's broad outcome

5. Hierarchical Classification Pipeline
The core architectural decision in Milestone 2 is a two-stage hierarchical pipeline. Rather than training a single
model to distinguish all four classes simultaneously (which was observed to produce ~56% accuracy for
Decision Trees), the problem is decomposed into two easier subproblems:
Stage 1 — Binary Classifier (Pass vs Fail)
All four raw labels are collapsed to two: Fail (includes Withdrawn) and Pass (includes Good, Very Good,
Excellent). This is an inherently easier problem with clear feature separation and achieves 83.82% accuracy.
Three models are evaluated: ExtraTrees, HistGBM (tuned), and Random Forest. HistGBM is selected as the
Stage 1 champion.
Model
CV Accuracy
Notes
ExtraTrees
~0.82
class_weight='balanced', n_estimators=200
HistGBM
~0.838
CHAMPION — tuned via RandomizedSearchCV, n_iter=40
RandomForest
~0.82
class_weight='balanced', n_estimators=200
Stage 2 — 4-class Classifier with Coarse Feature Injection
The Stage 1 binary prediction (coarse_pred = 0 or 1) is appended as an additional numeric feature to the
original feature matrix. Stage 2 then trains on this augmented matrix to predict the fine-grained 4-class label.
cross_val_predict is used on the training set (not predict) to generate out-of-fold Stage 1 predictions — this is
critical to prevent leakage where an overfitted Stage 1 provides artificially clean predictions to Stage 2.
Stage 2 Ultimate — Advanced Feature Engineering + Noise Dropping
An enhanced Stage 2 pipeline adds five new features (avg_hw_score, clicks_early, clicks_late,
avg_days_late_TMA, avg_days_late_CMA) and drops seven noisy columns identified as low-signal for 4-class
discrimination. Three models are tuned with RandomizedSearchCV (n_iter=5, cv=3):
Model
Best CV Acc
Best Params
ExtraTrees
0.7662
n_estimators=100, min_samples_split=5, max_depth=None

0.7870
n_estimators=100, min_samples_leaf=2, max_depth=15

0.8042

6.3 Improvement Over Baseline
The hierarchical pipeline with the ultimate Stage 2 represents a substantial improvement at each stage:

6.4 Confusion Matrix Analysis
From the Stage 2 confusion matrix with 6,519 test samples:
 Fail class: 3,057 correctly classified out of 3,447 (88.7% recall). Only 140 Fail students misclassified as
Very Good and 234 as Good — suggesting a subset of failing students still maintained moderate VLE
engagement.

 Very Good class: 1,121 / 1,337 correct (83.8% recall). Best-recalled passing class, confirming that the
feature set clearly captures the consistent, high-engagement student profile.
 Good class: 776 / 1,122 correct (69.1% recall). Most confusion is with Very Good (73 students), indicating
the Good/Very Good boundary is the hardest to separate.
 Excellent class: Only 270 / 613 correct (44.0% recall). This is the hardest class — Distinction students are a
small minority and their behaviour partially overlaps with Very Good students.

7.3 Key Technical Design Decisions
Decision
Rationale
Hierarchical 2-stage pipeline
Flat 4-class models achieved only ~67% accuracy. Decomposing into binary (Stage 1) + fine-grained (Stage 2) improved accuracy by 13%. Stage 1 coarse_pred is the 3rd most important feature in Stage 2.
cross_val_predict for Stage 1 train predictions
Using predict() directly would cause leakage — Stage 1 would have memorised training data, giving artificially perfect coarse predictions to Stage 2 during training. cross_val_predict generates out-of-fold predictions that simulate true test-time behaviour.
HistGradientBoostingClassifier as champion
10-50x faster than GradientBoostingClassifier (histogram binning like LightGBM). Native missing value handling. Built-in early stopping. Achieved highest CV accuracy in both Stage 1 (0.838) and Stage 2 (0.804).
RobustScaler instead of MinMaxScaler
Total_click is heavily right-skewed (outliers up to 25,000+). RobustScaler uses median/IQR instead of min/max, making it robust to such outliers for distance-based models (SVC, LR).
Dropping noisy features in Stage 2
studied_credits, module_presentation_length, num_of_prev_attempts, disability, age_band, gender, total_click are low-signal for 4-class distinction. Replacing them with avg_hw_score, clicks_early/late, avg_days_late improved CV accuracy from 0.70 to 0.80.
preprocess_df() reusable function
All imputation (imd_band regional mode, date_registration median) is encapsulated in one function that accepts fit=True (learns from train) or fit=False (applies learned stats to test/inference). reg_date_median and imd_mode_map are saved in preprocessing_bundle.pkl for deployment.
add_four_class_target() with drop-after
fail_rate and late_rate are used only to assign the Very Good label, then immediately dropped. This prevents a degenerate model that learns 'fail_rate==0 → Very Good' without understanding the actual academic features.

7.5 Inference Pipeline
The predict_from_raw_csvs() function accepts the 6 raw CSV files directly and returns a prediction DataFrame
with no preprocessing required from the caller:
Step
Action
1
Load all CSV files (accepts file paths or already-loaded DataFrames)
2
Build total_click from studentVle + vle (if provided; else 0)
3
Build fail_rate, late_rate, performance_level from assessment submission records
4
Rebuild regCoursesInfo from reg + courses + info (studentInfo) tables
5
Merge everything into one feature matrix
6
preprocess_df() with training-set statistics from preprocessing_bundle.pkl
7
add_four_class_target() → drop leaky columns (fail_rate, late_rate)
8
Enforce column types (str for categoricals, float for numerics)
9
Stage 1: s1_model.predict(X) → coarse_pred
10
Stage 2: s2_model.predict(X + coarse_pred) → final 4-class prediction + probabilities
11
Return DataFrame: id_student, code_module, code_presentation, stage1_coarse, predicted_class, proba_*

8. Discussion & Conclusion

8.2 Key Findings
 The hierarchical approach is definitively better than flat 4-class classification: +13% accuracy over a flat
ExtraTrees baseline by injecting Stage 1's binary prediction as a feature.
 The Excellent class remains the hardest to predict (44% recall) because Distinction students form a small
minority (9.4%) and behaviourally overlap significantly with Very Good students.
 VLE engagement (total_click) is the most predictive feature in Milestone 2, confirming Milestone 1's finding
that behavioural features outperform demographics.
 The module identity (code_module) is the second strongest predictor — different modules have
fundamentally different pass rates, suggesting per-module models could further improve accuracy.
 The coarse_pred feature from Stage 1 ranks 3rd in permutation importance, validating the hierarchical
decomposition: giving Stage 2 a binary prior dramatically sharpens its 4-class predictions.
 Advanced time-aware features (clicks_early vs clicks_late) and per-assessment-type late submission
averages provided the +9% lift from Stage 2 basic (0.70) to Stage 2 ultimate (0.80).
8.3 Limitations
 Excellent class recall is only 44% — the model struggles to identify the top-performing students reliably. A
dedicated Excellent vs Very Good classifier or SMOTE oversampling could address this.
 The Very Good class is defined by a threshold on fail_rate/late_rate, which are dropped before training. The
model infers Very Good indirectly from VLE behaviour and module identity.
 No temporal features (time-series of daily clicks) are used — a recurrent or sequence model capturing the
trajectory of engagement could substantially improve predictions.
 The inference pipeline requires access to the original studentVle.csv to build total_click — without it, click
features default to 0 and accuracy would be lower.
8.4 Next Steps
 XGBoost or LightGBM with native categorical support could further improve Stage 2 accuracy.
 A dedicated Stage 3 model separating Excellent from Very Good students (binary sub-classifier) could
improve Excellent recall from 44% to 70%+.

 Per-module separate models — since code_module is the 2nd strongest predictor, training one model per
module could capture module-specific patterns.
 Time-series features: rolling click counts per week, early-semester engagement slope, assignment
completion rate over time.
 SMOTE or class-weight tuning specifically for the Excellent minority class.
Final Champion: Hierarchical HistGBM — Stage 1: 83.82% binary accuracy | Stage 2: 80.13% 4-class
 accuracy

The objective of the projects is to prepare you to apply different machine learning 
algorithms to real -world tasks. This will help you to increase your knowledge 
about the workflow of the machine learning tasks. You will learn how to apply 
pre-processing, feature engineering, regression, and classification methods.

➢ In the practical exam :

for classification.

takes the new csv file, loads the saved models,  and outputs 
predictions. This is to allow us to test your model without re -
training.

models.

sample. (You can’t drop an entire test sample row).
